text:
In the latest of our daily interviews with the athletes heading forParis, we sat down with Olympic and two-time world silver medalist Keely Hodgkinson to chat about what her sport means to her.

Middle-distance runner Keely Hodgkinson burst on to theOlympicsscene at the age of 19, winning silver at the 2020 Tokyo games in the 800m final. In doing so, the Manchester-born athlete clocked an incredible new British timing of 1:55.88 – breaking the British record set byKelly Holmesin 1995.

Following on from her Olympic success, the 22-year-old went on to secure numerous other medals, including silver both in the 2022 World Championships in Eugene, and in Budapest a year later.

Having successfully defended her gold 800 metre title at the European Athletics Championships in Rome last month, despite falling ill just before the race, Hodgkinson has become one of the favourites to bag the top spot at the Paris games. And luck is on her side after her Tokyo Olympics rival defending champion Athing Mu recently fell in theUStrials and failed to qualify for this year’s games.

It is here where she will again come up against Kenyan Mary Moraa, who beat her at last year’s World Championships and the 2022Commonwealth Games, in pursuit of gold. Below, we spoke to Hodgkinson about her career and her thoughts on the upcoming games.

‘It has to be watching Jessica Ennis-Hill take gold at the 2012 London games. I was around ten years old and remember watching her win the race on the television and being so excited.

‘I had taken a break from the athletics club I was at but watching Jess win totally inspired me to return. I told my coach at the time that she was the reason I wanted to return to athletics. I have met her a couple of times and she is so lovely.’

‘With athletics you are always chasing the high. It has a very exciting element to it as you can train for so long and then on the day of an event you really don’t know what can happen.

‘You can get knocked down or succeed but it is so rewarding when all your effort pays off. There can be lows of course but we learn how to remain level-headed in this sport.’

‘It is definitely the training. What we do is relentless as I train six days a week for up to nine hours and only have one day off. Sometimes I have to train twice a day as we need to fit in gym work too. I work hard and have learnt a lot about my body and what it can do and the limits I can push it too.’

‘Fortunately these days there are athletic clubs all over the country so you can trial out what you want to do by trying a variety of different sports.

‘I have always been active as I was swimming from the age of seven and then got in to athletics a couple of years later but the good thing about my sport is that you can also come to it later in life. I know people who started at the age of 20 and are now pro.’

‘I didn’t actually know until the day I won. I had obviously thought about it but until I got my silver medal at the Tokyo games it was just something I dreamt of. And then after that success, I was so fortunate to progress rapidly in my career.’

‘It was definitely ten years ago when I had a tumour in my ear and had to have it operated on. The growth was found to be sitting very near my brain so it was a pretty scary procedure and I had to have a month off school and take time out from exercising, so that was difficult.

‘Although it wasn’t cancerous, the tumour had to be cut out and it has left me half deaf. Mentally that time was the hardest point for me.’

‘I am so excited to get there and am confident, particularly after my previous wins, I will bring home a medal for Team GB. I can’t wait to race in front of my mum and dad, my two sisters and brother who will all be there cheering me on. I am pretty confident I will do well.’

title: Keely Hodgkinson won Olympic silver aged 19 - she's going for Gold in Paris | Metro News
description: In the latest of our interviews with athletes at Paris 2024, we sat down with Olympic and two-time world silver medalist Keely Hodgkinson.
